author: @jorgemartin7697
text:
Dear Robert 
I would like to ask you a question, which does not have much to do with the subject of the video, but it is also very interesting
-By a farmer friend, I knew that rainwater is better for watering plants, because apparently it contains atmospheric nitrogen dissolved by the action of lightning and storms, and I thought
-Could this process be easily imitated at home?
using a tesla coil for example and an atmosphere saturated with moisture, would the electric discharge fix the nitrogen in the water in some way?
thank you Robert

replies.author: @ancapftw9113
replies.text:
Lightning naturally produces nitrogen oxide, which reacts with oxygen in the air to form nitrogen dioxide.  This reacts with water to form nitric acid, which reacts with a large number of minerals to form nitrates.

Electric arcs can be substituted for lightning.  Cody's Lab did a video on it.

replies.author: @TonyGrant.
replies.text: You don't need to fix the nitrogen, you just need to dissolve it into the droplet. If you can charge the droplet it will attract the neutrally charged N2 molecule (and other gasses) as it falls. I have wanted to do this exact experiment for a couple of decades now but using kelvin's thunderstorm to put the charge onto the droplets. Check out Kelvin's Thunderstorm.
